title: Pharmacokinetics
summary: الحرائك الدوائية (Pharmacokinetics) تدرس ما يفعله الجسم بالدواء عبر أربع مراحل: الامتصاص (Absorption)، التوزيع (Distribution)، الاستقلاب (Metabolism)، والإطراح (Excretion) - ADME. الامتصاص يتأثر بطريقة التناول والتأين والذوبانية الدهنية، بينما التوزيع يعتمد على حجم التوزيع (Vd) والارتباط بالبروتينات البلازمية. يحدث الاستقلاب الكبدي على مرحلتين (Phase I و II)، وقد يتأثر بمحرضات أو مثبطات إنزيمية. تُقاس سرعة الإطراح بنصف العمر (t1/2) الذي يستغرق 4-5 منه للوصول للحالة المستقرة.

key_drugs.name: Morphine
key_drugs.note: يخضع لـ First-pass metabolism كبير، لذلك فاعليته الفموية أقل بكثير من IV

key_drugs.name: Propranolol
key_drugs.note: First-pass metabolism عالي، يحتاج جرعات فموية أعلى لتعادل IV

key_drugs.name: Nitroglycerin
key_drugs.note: يُعطى تحت اللسان (Sublingual) لتجنب First-pass metabolism

key_drugs.name: Warfarin
key_drugs.note: ارتباط عالٍ ببروتينات البلازما (>90%)، يتنافس مع Aspirin على الارتباط

key_drugs.name: Phenytoin
key_drugs.note: يسير بـ Zero-order kinetics عند جرعات عالية، t1/2 غير ثابت

key_drugs.name: Isoniazid (INH)
key_drugs.note: يُستقلب بالـ Acetylation - fast vs slow acetylators (Genetic polymorphism)

key_drugs.name: Rifampin
key_drugs.note: محرض إنزيمي قوي (Enzyme Inducer) لـ CYP - يُقلل تأثير الأدوية الأخرى

key_drugs.name: Ketoconazole
key_drugs.note: مثبط إنزيمي (Enzyme Inhibitor) - يزيد تركيز الأدوية التي تُستقلب بنفس الإنزيم

mnemonics.title: مراحل الحرائك الدوائية: ADME
mnemonics.body:
A = Absorption (الامتصاص)
D = Distribution (التوزيع)
M = Metabolism (الاستقلاب)
E = Excretion (الإطراح)
تذكر: الدواء يمر بـ ADME داخل الجسم

mnemonics.title: الامتصاص: غير مشحون = يُمتص
mnemonics.body: تخيل الدواء يريد اجتياز جدار الأمعاء. الجدار كاره للماء (Lipophilic). الدواء المشحون (Ionized) = يحمل حقيبة ثقيلة (Water soluble) - لا يستطيع العبور. الدواء غير المشحون (Unionized) = خفيف (Lipid soluble) - يعبر بسهولة. القاعدة: Unionized = Lipid soluble = More absorbed.

mnemonics.title: First-Pass Metabolism: حاجز الكبد
mnemonics.body: تخيل دواءً فمويًا يسافر: يُبتلع، يُمتص من الأمعاء، ثم يصطدم بـ 'نقطة تفتيش' الكبد. الكبد يُفكك جزءاً منه قبل أن يصل الدم. النتيجة: Bioavailability < 100%. Morphine وPropranolol يخسران كثيراً عند هذه النقطة. Nitroglycerin يتجاوزها بالطريق تحت اللساني.

mnemonics.title: عوامل تؤثر على توزيع الدواء: PLBR
mnemonics.body:
P = Plasma protein binding (الارتباط ببروتينات البلازما)
L = Lipid solubility (الذوبانية الدهنية)
B = Blood-brain barrier (حاجز الدماغ)
R = Regional blood flow (تدفق الدم الإقليمي)
PLBR = كل ما يحدد أين يذهب الدواء

mnemonics.title: نصف العمر t1/2: قاعدة الأنصاف
mnemonics.body: تخيل 100 جندي دواء في الجسم. بعد t1/2 الأول: يبقى 50. بعد الثاني: 25. بعد الثالث: 12.5. بعد الرابع: 6.25. بعد الخامس: ~3.1. بعد 4-5 نصف عمر: الجيش انتهى تقريباً. نفس المبدأ لـ Steady State: تحتاج 4-5 t1/2 للوصول إليه.

flashcards.front: What is Bioavailability? When is it 100%?
flashcards.back: It is the fraction of a dose that reaches the systemic circulation. It is 100% only with intravenous (IV) administration. The oral route is always less than 100% due to incomplete absorption and first-pass metabolism.

flashcards.front: What is First-Pass Metabolism and what is its clinical significance?
flashcards.back: Metabolism of a drug in the liver and intestinal wall before it reaches systemic circulation. It reduces bioavailability. Examples: Morphine, Propranolol. Solution: alternative routes such as sublingual (Nitroglycerin).

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between ionization and absorption?
flashcards.back: Only the unionized form is absorbed because it is lipid-soluble. The ionized form is water-soluble and cannot penetrate the cell membrane.

flashcards.front: What is Volume of Distribution (Vd)? What does a high value mean?
flashcards.back: A hypothetical volume that reflects the distribution of a drug throughout the body. Vd = D / C. High Vd = drug distributes extensively into tissues outside the blood. Low Vd = drug remains in plasma.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between the free and protein-bound forms of a drug in plasma?
flashcards.back: Free form: pharmacologically active, diffusible, metabolized and excreted. Protein-bound form: inactive, non-diffusible, not metabolized or excreted — acts as a reservoir.

flashcards.front: Why does Aspirin increase the risk of bleeding when given with Warfarin?
flashcards.back: Aspirin has a higher affinity for plasma proteins and displaces Warfarin from its binding sites, increasing the free fraction of Warfarin and enhancing its anticoagulant effect.

flashcards.front: What are Phase I reactions in drug metabolism?
flashcards.back: Non-synthetic reactions: oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis. Carried out by Cytochrome P450 enzymes. Convert the drug into a more polar metabolite.

flashcards.front: What are Phase II reactions in drug metabolism?
flashcards.back: Conjugation reactions with glucuronic acid, sulfate, or acetate. Most common: glucuronidation. Result: water-soluble compounds that are easily excreted by the kidneys.

flashcards.front: What is Enzyme Induction and what is its effect on drug levels?
flashcards.back: Induction of metabolic enzymes (e.g., CYP3A4) increases the metabolism rate of affected drugs, lowering their blood levels and reducing therapeutic effect. Example: Rifampin induces CYP → reduces efficacy of oral contraceptives.

flashcards.front: What is Enzyme Inhibition and what is its effect on drug levels?
flashcards.back: Inhibition of metabolic enzymes slows drug metabolism, raising drug concentration in the blood and increasing the risk of toxicity. Example: Ketoconazole inhibits CYP → raises levels of co-administered drugs.

flashcards.front: What is the half-life (t½)? How is it calculated?
flashcards.back: The time required for the drug concentration to fall by 50%. t½ = 0.693 × Vd / CL. It determines the time to reach steady state.

flashcards.front: How many t½ are required to reach Steady State?
flashcards.back: 4–5 half-lives are required to reach steady state and likewise for complete elimination.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between First-order and Zero-order kinetics?
flashcards.back:
First-order: elimination rate is proportional to concentration — a constant fraction is eliminated. t½ is constant. Most drugs.
Zero-order: a constant amount is eliminated regardless of concentration. t½ is not constant. Examples: Phenytoin, Ethanol.

flashcards.front: What is the Loading Dose equation?
flashcards.back: Loading Dose (LD) = Vd × Target Cp. Given when a rapid therapeutic concentration is needed.

flashcards.front: What is the Maintenance Dose equation?
flashcards.back: Maintenance Dose = Css × CL. (Css = Steady state concentration, CL = Clearance). Maintains the steady-state concentration.

flashcards.front: What are the mechanisms of absorption from the gastrointestinal tract?
flashcards.back:
1. Passive diffusion (most common)
2. Facilitated diffusion
3. Active transport
4. Endocytosis

flashcards.front: What is the effect of liver disease on drug distribution?
flashcards.back: Severe liver cirrhosis → decreased albumin → increased free fraction of protein-bound drugs → exaggerated pharmacological effect and adverse reactions.

flashcards.front: What is the effect of renal failure on drugs?
flashcards.back: Reduces renal elimination of drugs → drug accumulation → increased risk of toxicity. Example: Digoxin accumulates in renal failure.

flashcards.front: What are the routes of drug excretion from the body?
flashcards.back: Kidneys (most important), bile, lungs (volatile agents), intestines, breast milk. The kidneys excrete drugs via: filtration, active secretion, and reabsorption.

flashcards.front: Why does Nitroglycerin require sublingual administration?
flashcards.back: If given orally it would undergo extensive first-pass metabolism in the liver, abolishing its efficacy. Sublingual administration allows direct absorption into systemic circulation bypassing the liver.

flashcards.front: What are the stages of pharmacokinetics (ADME)?
flashcards.back: Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion.

flashcards.front: What is the most common mechanism of drug absorption?
flashcards.back: Passive diffusion of lipid-soluble, unionized drugs.

flashcards.front: Which form of a drug is absorbed: ionized or unionized?
flashcards.back: Unionized, because it is lipid-soluble and crosses membranes.

flashcards.front: What is Bioavailability?
flashcards.back: The fraction of a dose that effectively reaches systemic circulation; IV = 100%.

flashcards.front: What is Volume of Distribution (Vd)?
flashcards.back: Vd = Dose / Plasma concentration; a hypothetical volume relating the amount of drug to its plasma concentration.

flashcards.front: What is Clearance?
flashcards.back: The volume of plasma cleared of drug per unit time; determines the maintenance dose.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between t½, Clearance, and Vd?
flashcards.back: t½ = 0.693 × Vd / CL.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between zero-order and first-order kinetics?
flashcards.back: Zero-order: a constant amount is metabolized per unit time (e.g., alcohol, aspirin at high doses). First-order: a constant fraction is metabolized per unit time (most drugs).

flashcards.front: What are the two phases of hepatic drug metabolism?
flashcards.back: Phase I (oxidation/reduction/hydrolysis via CYP450) and Phase II (conjugation — e.g., glucuronidation).

flashcards.front: How does liver disease affect pharmacokinetics (PK)?
flashcards.back: Decreased albumin, hepatic blood flow, and enzyme activity → reduced metabolism and protein binding → adverse effects.

mcqs.q: What is the correct definition of Bioavailability?
options: The fraction of drug bound to proteins
The fraction of the dose that reaches the systemic circulation
The rate of drug absorption from the intestine
The hypothetical volume of drug distribution
mcqs.explanation: Bioavailability = the fraction of the dose that reaches systemic circulation. It is 100% with IV administration and decreases with oral administration due to incomplete absorption and first-pass metabolism.

mcqs.q: Which form of a drug is best absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract?
mcqs.explanation: The unionized form is lipophilic and easily penetrates cell membranes. The ionized form is water-soluble and is poorly absorbed.

mcqs.q: What is the main reason Nitroglycerin is given sublingually rather than orally?
options: To increase speed of absorption only
To bypass first-pass metabolism in the liver
Because it is insoluble in water
Because the gastrointestinal tract degrades it
mcqs.explanation: Nitroglycerin undergoes extensive first-pass metabolism in the liver which abolishes its efficacy if given orally. Sublingual administration bypasses the liver and enters systemic circulation directly.

mcqs.q: A drug with Vd = 100 L means:
options: The drug is confined to plasma only
The drug distributes extensively into tissues outside the plasma
The drug is highly bound to plasma proteins
mcqs.explanation: A high Vd means the drug distributes widely outside the vasculature into tissues. A low Vd (e.g., plasma volume ~3–5 L) means the drug remains in the blood.

mcqs.q: What are Phase I reactions in drug metabolism?
options: Binding of the drug to albumin
Filtration of the drug in the kidneys
mcqs.explanation: Phase I = Oxidation, Reduction, Hydrolysis. Carried out by Cytochrome P450 enzymes. They increase drug polarity to facilitate excretion.

mcqs.q: Rifampin reduces the efficacy of oral contraceptives because:
options: It inhibits their absorption from the intestine
It induces CYP enzymes, increasing their metabolism
It inhibits renal excretion of the contraceptives
mcqs.explanation: Rifampin is a potent enzyme inducer. It induces CYP enzymes → increases metabolism of oral contraceptives → lowers their blood levels → therapeutic failure (unintended pregnancy).

mcqs.q: A drug following zero-order kinetics — which statement is correct?
options: t½ is constant regardless of dose
Steady state is reached in 4–5 t½
A constant fraction of drug is eliminated per unit time
A constant amount is eliminated per unit time — t½ is not constant
mcqs.explanation: Zero-order kinetics: a constant amount is eliminated regardless of concentration (saturable process). t½ is not constant. There is no true steady state. Examples: Phenytoin, Ethanol.

mcqs.q: What is the Loading Dose equation?
mcqs.explanation: Loading Dose = Vd × Target Plasma Concentration. Given to achieve therapeutic concentration rapidly without waiting 4–5 t½.

mcqs.q: A patient has renal failure — which drug is most at risk for accumulation?
options: Digoxin, which is primarily excreted via the kidneys
Drugs that undergo Phase II reactions
mcqs.explanation: Digoxin is primarily excreted by the kidneys. In renal failure its excretion decreases → accumulation → increased risk of toxicity. Dose adjustment is required.

mcqs.q: After how many t½ is a drug essentially eliminated from the body?
mcqs.explanation: After 4–5 half-lives: 1st=50%, 2nd=75%, 3rd=87.5%, 4th=93.75%, 5th=~97% eliminated. Therefore 4–5 t½ is sufficient for complete elimination and to reach steady state.
Passive diffusion is the predominant mechanism.
Zero-order: a constant amount per unit time.
